sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] not in ['B', 'W', 'c', 'G']:
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']),
                  sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
        if center[0] > 5:
            print(f"Node: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, center~({center[0]:.0f},{center[1]:.0f}), children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The magenta block moved from (44, 42) to (40, 40)! So clicking at (40, 40) did move the block. Previously I thought it didn't move because I was checking the wrong frame or the block was still at (44, 42). But now it's at (40, 40).
So the movement mechanic works: clicking at a position moves the closest block to that position. The block at (44, 42) moved to (40, 40), which is distance sqrt(4^2 + 2^2) = sqrt(20) ≈ 4.5.
Now I need to move this block closer to the blob and then absorb it. The blob is at (27, 33). Let me move the magenta block at (40, 40) to a position adjacent to the blob, like (32, 36).
Distance from (40, 40) to (32, 36): sqrt(8^2 + 4^2) = sqrt(80) ≈ 8.9
Distance from (56, 16) to (32, 36): sqrt(24^2 + 20^2) = sqrt(976) ≈ 31.2
The purple block is inside the blob at (27, 33). So the closest block to (32, 36) is the magenta block at (40, 40). Let me try clicking at (32, 36).
But wait, I also need to consider the distance limit. The block moved from (44, 42) to (40, 40), which is about 4.5 pixels. Can it move 8.9 pixels in one click? Looking at earlier moves, blocks moved up to about 8-9 pixels. Let me try.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move magenta block at (40, 40) closer to blob: click (32, 36)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 32, 'col': 36}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
